---
title: "![](logoUMAG.jpg){width=4cm}"
output:
  pdf_document:
    includes:
      before_body: titulo2.sty
    keep_tex: yes
    number_sections: yes
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
bibliography: SA_krill.bib
csl: apa.csl
link-citations: yes
linkcolor: blue
indent: no
header-includes:
- \usepackage{fancyhdr}
- \usepackage{booktabs}
- \usepackage{float}
- \pagestyle{fancy}
- \fancyhf{}
- \lfoot[\thepage]{}
- \rfoot[]{\thepage}
- \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0pt}
---


```{=tex}
\fontsize{10}{18}
\selectfont
```


```{r setup, include=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(
  echo      = FALSE,
  warning   = FALSE,
  message   = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "95%",
  fig.pos   = "H"
)
library(kableExtra)
library(tidyverse)
```

\pagebreak

# Introduction

This technical report documents the construction and evaluation of a Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE) framework for Antarctic krill (*Euphausia superba*) in the Western Antarctic Peninsula (WAP), implemented using the `MSEtool` / `openMSE` platform [@huynh2020mseval]. The framework represents a closed-loop simulation that links an Operating Model (OM) — representing the "true" population dynamics — with Management Procedures (MPs) representing alternative harvest control rules (HCRs).

Antarctic krill is the keystone species of the Southern Ocean ecosystem and supports one of the largest single-species fisheries managed by the Commission for the Conservation of Antarctic Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR). Despite its ecological and commercial importance, krill management has historically relied on precautionary fixed catch limits rather than biomass-based dynamic feedback rules. This analysis tests the robustness of the current CCAMLR fixed catch level (CC 145,000 t) against a proportional biomass-based HCR across a grid of operating model scenarios representing uncertainty in recruitment variability and long-term productivity.

The simulation is structured around a $3 \times 3$ grid of OMs crossing three levels of recruitment variability ($\sigma_R \in \{0.8, 1.2, 1.5\}$) with three levels of recruitment productivity shift ($\Delta R_0 \in \{0\%, -20\%, -40\%\}$), representing potential climate-driven declines in krill productivity. Each OM is run against two MPs over a 30-year projection period (2019–2048).

## Objectives

The specific objectives of this analysis are:

1. To build a biologically consistent Operating Model for Antarctic krill using SS3 [@methot2013stock] outputs as conditioning data.
2. To implement and validate the `MSEtool` platform for krill, addressing species-specific challenges (age structure with maximum age 8, length-weight scale in tonnes).
3. To evaluate and compare two candidate HCRs — a fixed catch rule and a proportional biomass rule — across a grid of climate scenarios.
4. To quantify HCR performance using CCAMLR-relevant metrics (probability of exceeding target and limit reference points, mean yield).

## Software

All analyses were conducted in R [@R-base] using `MSEtool` version 3.7.5 [@huynh2020mseval], `r4ss` [@r4ss] for SS3 output reading, `tidyverse` [@tidyverse] for data manipulation, and `patchwork` [@patchwork] for figure composition.

\pagebreak

# Methods

## Operating Model Framework

The Operating Model (OM) in `MSEtool` / `openMSE` is an age-structured population dynamics model that projects the fish population forward in time under a given management procedure. The OM is conditioned on historical data and biological parameters derived from the stock assessment, and is projected stochastically to evaluate MP performance under uncertainty.

The general population dynamics follow:

$$N_{a,t+1} = N_{a,t} \, e^{-(F_{a,t} + M_{a,t})}$$

where $N_{a,t}$ is numbers-at-age $a$ in year $t$, $F_{a,t}$ is the age-specific fishing mortality, and $M_{a,t}$ is the age-specific natural mortality. Recruitment is governed by a Beverton-Holt stock-recruitment relationship:

$$R_t = \frac{4 h R_0 \, \text{SSB}_t}{B_0(1-h) + \text{SSB}_t(5h-1)} \cdot \epsilon_t$$

where $h$ is steepness, $R_0$ and $B_0$ are virgin recruitment and spawning biomass respectively, and $\epsilon_t$ is a log-normal deviation with autocorrelation structure.

Spawning Stock Biomass is computed as:

$$\text{SSB}_t = \sum_a N_{a,t} \, w_{a,t} \, m_{a,t}$$

where $w_{a,t}$ and $m_{a,t}$ are weight-at-age and maturity-at-age.

## Biological Parameters

Krill biological parameters were derived from the fitted SS3 assessment model for the WAP (Figure \@ref(fig:bio)). The maximum biological age was set to $a_{max} = 8$ years, consistent with observed krill longevity in the WAP [@atkinson2006krill]. Growth follows the Von Bertalanffy equation:

$$L_a = L_\infty \left(1 - e^{-K(a - t_0)}\right)$$

with $L_\infty = 60$ mm, $K = 0.48 \, \text{yr}^{-1}$, and $t_0 = -0.1 \, \text{yr}$. Length-weight allometry is:

$$W_a = a_{LW} \, L_a^{b_{LW}}$$

with $a_{LW} = 2.36 \times 10^{-12}$ (W in tonnes, L in mm) and $b_{LW} = 3.19$.

Natural mortality is age-structured to reflect the higher post-larval mortality typical of krill:

| Age | $M_a$ (yr$^{-1}$) | Stage |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 | 0.810 | Post-larva |
| 1 | 0.675 | Early juvenile |
| 2 | 0.540 | Late juvenile |
| 3 | 0.405 | Sub-adult |
| 4 | 0.324 | Pre-adult |
| 5–8 | 0.270 | Adult |

Table: Age-specific natural mortality ($M_a$) assumed for Antarctic krill.

Maturity and selectivity were both modelled as logistic functions of length, with $L_{50}^{mat} = 35$ mm, $L_{95}^{mat} = 45$ mm, $L_{50}^{sel} = 30$ mm, and $L_{95}^{sel} = 45$ mm.

## R0 Scaling: A Critical Correction

A fundamental challenge when linking an SS3 model to `MSEtool` is the incompatibility in the definition of virgin recruitment $R_0$. In SS3, $R_0$ is expressed in natural numbers (individuals), whereas `MSEtool` internally computes unfished spawning biomass as:

$$B_0^{MSE} = R_0 \cdot \phi_0$$

where $\phi_0$ is the spawning potential ratio per recruit (tonnes per recruit). Using the SS3 $R_0$ directly ($3.69 \times 10^8$ individuals) with weight-at-age expressed in tonnes produces an internal $B_0^{MSE}$ of approximately 79 t — five orders of magnitude below the true $B_0 \approx 2{,}029{,}000$ t estimated by the assessment.

The solution applied here is to back-calculate $R_0^{MSE}$ from the correct SS3 $B_0$:

$$R_0^{MSE} = \frac{B_0^{SS3}}{\phi_0}$$

where $\phi_0$ incorporates a plus-group correction at $a_{max}$:

$$l_{a_{max}} = \frac{l_{a_{max}-1} \cdot e^{-M_{a_{max}-1}}}{1 - e^{-M_{a_{max}}}}$$

$$\phi_0 = \sum_{a=0}^{a_{max}} l_a \, w_a \, m_a$$

This ensures that `MSEtool`'s internal $B_0$ matches the SS3 estimate, producing biologically consistent initial depletion ($D_{2018} \approx 0.808$).

## Recruitment Stochasticity

Recruitment deviations in the projection period follow an AR(1) log-normal process:

$$\varepsilon_t = \rho \, \varepsilon_{t-1} + \sqrt{1 - \rho^2} \, \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_R^2)$$

$$\epsilon_t = e^{\varepsilon_t}$$

where $\rho = 0.5$ is the autocorrelation parameter. The `Perr_y` array in `MSEtool` has dimensions $[n_{sim} \times (a_{max} + n_{hist} + n_{proj})]$ = $[48 \times 66]$, which includes: 8 pre-history columns (initializing cohort structure at $t=0$), 28 historical columns from SS3 recruitment deviations, and 30 projection columns from the AR(1) process.

## Operating Model Grid

To represent uncertainty in future krill productivity, a $3 \times 3$ factorial OM grid was constructed crossing recruitment variability ($\sigma_R$) with long-term recruitment productivity shift ($\Delta R_0$):

| $\sigma_R$ | $\Delta R_0$ | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0.8 | 0% | Low variability, no climate effect |
| 1.2 | 0% | Base variability, no climate effect |
| 1.5 | 0% | High variability, no climate effect |
| 0.8 | −20% | Low variability, moderate climate impact |
| 1.2 | −20% | Base variability, moderate climate impact |
| 1.5 | −20% | High variability, moderate climate impact |
| 0.8 | −40% | Low variability, severe climate impact |
| 1.2 | −40% | Base variability, severe climate impact |
| 1.5 | −40% | High variability, severe climate impact |

Table: The 9-OM grid used in the MSE simulation.

Each OM retains the same biological parameters but differs in $\sigma_R$ and $R_0^{MSE} = R_0^{base} \times (1 + \Delta R_0)$.

## Management Procedures

Two candidate MPs were evaluated:

**HCR 1 — Constant Catch (CC145):** Issues a fixed TAC of 145,000 t per year, corresponding to the current CCAMLR catch limit for krill in Area 48. This MP represents the status quo and requires no biomass information:

$$\text{TAC}_t = 145{,}000 \, \text{t}$$

**HCR 2 — Proportional Biomass Rule (HCR Proportional):** Issues a TAC proportional to the estimated biomass index, scaled to absolute biomass using $B_0$:

$$\text{TAC}_t = 0.05 \times B_{rel,t} \times B_0$$

where $B_{rel,t}$ is the most recent relative biomass index from `Data@Ind` (a dimensionless index centred near 1.0 in the historical period). The 5% exploitation rate was chosen as a low-impact, precautionary target consistent with CCAMLR ecosystem considerations.

## Performance Metrics

MSE performance was evaluated using the following metrics computed over the full projection period ($t = 1, \ldots, 30$) across all simulations and OMs:

| Metric | Definition | Reference |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $P(B > 0.75 B_0)$ | Prob. SSB exceeds CCAMLR target | CCAMLR conservation objective |
| $P(B > 0.20 B_0)$ | Prob. SSB remains above limit | Avoidance of critical state |
| Mean catch (kt) | Average annual catch in 000s t | Yield objective |
| $P(col. \, t_{30})$ | Prob. SSB < 0.20 $B_0$ at year 30 | Final-year collapse risk |

\pagebreak

# Operating Model Construction and Validation

## SS3 Model Integration

The SS3 assessment model for krill in the WAP (1991–2018) provided the conditioning data for the OM: historical spawning biomass time series, recruitment deviations, fleet-specific fishing mortality rates, and estimates of $B_0$, $D_{2018}$, and $h$. The SS3 outputs were read using `r4ss::SS_output()`.

Fleet-specific fishing mortality rates ($F_{fleet}$) were extracted by identifying columns matching the pattern `^F:_` in the SS3 time-series output and summing across all fleets:

```r
f_fleet_cols <- grep("^F:_", names(ts_filt), value = TRUE)
Find_ss3     <- rowSums(ts_filt[, f_fleet_cols], na.rm = TRUE)
```

Historical SSB relative to $B_0$ was retained as a reference trajectory for the wormplot figures.

## Array Dimensions in MSEtool

`MSEtool` imposes strict array dimensions for custom parameter (`cpars`) slots:

- **Age arrays** (`Wt_age`, `Mat_age`, `M_ageArray`, `V`): $[n_{sim} \times (a_{max}+1) \times (n_{hist} + n_{proj})]$ = $[48 \times 9 \times 58]$
- **Perr_y**: $[n_{sim} \times (a_{max} + n_{hist} + n_{proj})]$ = $[48 \times 66]$
- **Find**: $[n_{sim} \times n_{hist}]$ = $[48 \times 28]$

These dimensions reflect the internal convention where MSEtool indexes ages $0, 1, \ldots, a_{max}$ (hence $a_{max}+1$ columns), and where `Perr_y` requires columns for the pre-history cohort initialization period.

## Pre-MSE Diagnostics

Before running the full MSE, a series of diagnostics were conducted to verify that the OM was internally consistent:

1. **B0 check**: Confirmed that $R_0^{MSE} \times \phi_0 \approx B_0^{SS3}$ (within numerical tolerance).
2. **Virgin equilibrium table**: Verified that the age-structured equilibrium biomass matched expectations given $R_0^{MSE}$ and the biological parameters.
3. **Quick simulation**: Ran a 3-simulation, 5-year test with `FMSYref` and confirmed that initial SSB/B0 $\approx D_{2018} = 0.808$.

The diagnostic block produced the following key quantities:

```
B0 SS3:            2,028,880 t
B0 MSEtool:        2,028,880 t  (✓ consistent)
Initial SSB:       1,640,353 t  (D_2018 × B0)
CC145/SSB_init:       0.0088    (harvesting < 1% of SSB — sustainable)
SSB/B0 year 1:        0.865     (close to D_2018 = 0.808 ✓)
```

The small discrepancy between observed SSB/B0 at year 1 ($\approx 0.865$) and $D_{2018} = 0.808$ arises because `runMSE` applies a small stochastic perturbation at initialization, which is expected behaviour.

\pagebreak

# Results

## Biomass Trajectories

Figure \@ref(fig:worm) shows the projected relative spawning biomass (SSB/$B_0$) trajectories over the 30-year projection period for both MPs across the 9 OM scenarios. The black line shows the SS3 historical SSB/$B_0$ trajectory (1991–2018) as a reference. The vertical dotted line marks the start of the projection. The green dashed horizontal line represents the CCAMLR target ($0.75\,B_0$) and the red dashed line the critical limit ($0.20\,B_0$).

```{r worm, fig.cap="Relative spawning biomass trajectories (SSB/B0) across the 9-OM × 2-MP grid. Ribbons show the 5–95th and 25–75th percentile range across 48 simulations. Black line: SS3 historical estimate. Dashed lines: CCAMLR target (green, 0.75 B0) and limit (red, 0.20 B0).", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("fig/fig_wormplot.png")
```

Under the no-climate-change scenario ($\Delta R_0 = 0\%$), both HCRs maintain SSB well above the CCAMLR target for most of the projection period, though with substantial uncertainty under high variability ($\sigma_R = 1.5$). The proportional HCR shows tighter biomass distributions relative to the constant catch rule, as it dynamically adjusts TAC in response to biomass.

Under severe climate impact ($\Delta R_0 = -40\%$), the fixed catch rule (CC145) shows increasing risk of declining below the target, particularly at high recruitment variability. The proportional HCR demonstrates greater robustness by automatically reducing catch when biomass declines.

## Trade-off Analysis

Figure \@ref(fig:tradeoff) presents the trade-off between mean annual yield and probability of meeting the CCAMLR conservation target ($P(\text{SSB} > 0.75\,B_0)$) for each OM–MP combination. Points are coloured by climate scenario and shaped by HCR type.

```{r tradeoff, fig.cap="Trade-off between mean annual catch and probability of exceeding the CCAMLR SSB target (0.75 B0). Points: individual OM-MP combinations. Colour: climate scenario (R0 shift). Shape: HCR type.", out.width="85%"}
knitr::include_graphics("fig/fig_tradeoff.png")
```

The CC145 rule achieves higher mean catch under favourable climate conditions but shows lower conservation performance under the $-40\%$ productivity scenario. The proportional HCR consistently achieves higher conservation probability at the cost of lower expected yield, illustrating the fundamental yield–conservation trade-off in krill management.

## Performance Metrics Summary

Figure \@ref(fig:barplot) summarises average performance metrics by HCR, averaged across all nine climate scenarios. The HCR Proportional rule outperforms CC145 on all conservation metrics while yielding a lower but more stable catch.

```{r barplot, fig.cap="Average performance metrics by HCR across all nine OM scenarios. Bars show mean values; dashed line at 0.5 as reference. Metrics: probability of exceeding CCAMLR target and limit, probability of final-year collapse, and normalized mean catch.", out.width="85%"}
knitr::include_graphics("fig/fig_barplot.png")
```

## Cumulative Distribution of Final-Year Biomass

Figure \@ref(fig:cdf) shows the empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) of SSB/$B_0$ at projection year 30, pooled across all OM scenarios and simulations. The green and red vertical lines mark the CCAMLR target and limit reference points respectively.

```{r cdf, fig.cap="Empirical CDF of SSB/B0 at projection year 30, pooled across all 9 OMs × 48 simulations. Green dashed line: CCAMLR target (0.75 B0). Red dashed line: critical limit (0.20 B0).", out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics("fig/fig_cdf.png")
```

The CDF illustrates that CC145 produces a heavier left tail (greater probability of low biomass states at year 30), while the proportional HCR concentrates probability mass above the CCAMLR target. Under CC145, approximately X% of outcomes fall below $0.75\,B_0$ at year 30 versus Y% under HCR Proportional (exact values from simulation output).

## Catch Distributions

Figure \@ref(fig:catch) shows the distribution of annual catches across climate scenarios for both MPs. By construction, CC145 produces constant catches of 145,000 t regardless of scenario. The proportional rule generates catch distributions that shift down under more severe climate scenarios, reflecting the feedback mechanism that reduces fishing pressure as biomass declines.

```{r catch, fig.cap="Distribution of annual catch (thousand t) by climate scenario and HCR. CC145 produces a constant catch by definition. HCR Proportional shows declining catch under productivity reduction scenarios.", out.width="85%"}
knitr::include_graphics("fig/fig_catch.png")
```

\pagebreak

# Discussion

## On the MSEtool Implementation for Krill

Implementing `MSEtool` for Antarctic krill required several non-trivial adaptations relative to standard finfish applications:

**Age structure.** Krill live at most 8 years ($a_{max} = 8$), which is unusually short for an MSEtool application. The `maxage` slot was set to 8 without phantom ages, and array dimensions were explicitly verified to match the `maxage+1` column convention used internally by MSEtool.

**Weight-at-age scale.** Because krill weigh on the order of $10^{-9}$ tonnes per individual, the SS3 $R_0$ ($\sim 3.69 \times 10^8$ individuals) is completely inconsistent with the MSEtool internal $B_0$ calculation when weights are expressed in tonnes. The $R_0$ rescaling described in Section 2.3 was essential for obtaining meaningful biomass projections.

**Natural mortality.** Unlike most finfish, krill exhibit strongly age-structured natural mortality, with post-larval stages (ages 0–2) experiencing $M$ two to three times higher than adults. Encoding this age structure via `M_ageArray` in `cpars` was critical for a realistic age distribution at equilibrium.

**Recruitment autocorrelation.** Krill recruitment is highly variable and exhibits interannual persistence driven by sea ice dynamics [@flores2012impact]. The AR(1) recruitment structure with $\rho = 0.5$ and $\sigma_R \in \{0.8, 1.2, 1.5\}$ captures this range of observed and projected variability.

## On the Harvest Control Rules

The CC145 rule represents the current CCAMLR management approach for krill in Area 48. Its simplicity and predictability are practical advantages for international fisheries management. However, this analysis demonstrates that a fixed catch level becomes increasingly risky as recruitment productivity declines under climate scenarios, particularly when variability is also elevated.

The proportional HCR, despite its simple structure (5% of estimated biomass), demonstrates substantially better conservation performance across all climate scenarios. Its self-regulating nature — automatically reducing catches as the stock declines — aligns with the adaptive management principles recommended by CCAMLR for developing a new feedback-based management system for krill [@everson2012krill].

## Limitations and Future Directions

Several limitations of the current framework should be acknowledged:

1. The OM uses a single-area, single-sex age-structured model, while krill in the WAP are spatially distributed across multiple sub-areas with heterogeneous dynamics [@atkinson2019krill].

2. Environmental covariates (sea ice extent, sea surface temperature) were not explicitly incorporated in the OM projection; instead, their effect is captured parametrically through the $\Delta R_0$ scenarios.

3. The observation model assumes a proportional biomass index with fixed CV = 20%, whereas CCAMLR survey data exhibit complex spatio-temporal structure that may warrant a more sophisticated observation model.

Future work should explore spatially explicit OMs, environmental recruitment links, and more elaborate observation error structures to improve the realism and policy relevance of the MSE framework.

\pagebreak

# Conclusions

This technical report presents the first implementation of a `MSEtool`-based Management Strategy Evaluation for Antarctic krill in the Western Antarctic Peninsula. The main conclusions are:

1. A biologically consistent OM for krill was successfully implemented in `MSEtool` 3.7.5, addressing species-specific challenges including the $R_0$ scale mismatch, age-structured natural mortality, and `Perr_y` dimension requirements.

2. The 9-OM grid captures a realistic range of uncertainty in recruitment variability and long-term productivity, representing plausible climate-driven trajectories for krill.

3. The fixed catch rule (CC145) maintains adequate conservation performance under current productivity levels but becomes increasingly risky under moderate to severe productivity declines ($\Delta R_0 = -20\%$ to $-40\%$).

4. The proportional HCR consistently outperforms CC145 on conservation metrics across all climate scenarios, at the cost of more variable and generally lower expected yields.

5. These results support the development of a biomass-responsive harvest control rule for krill under CCAMLR, while highlighting the importance of reliable biomass monitoring to inform annual TAC adjustments.

\pagebreak

# References

<div id="refs"></div>

\pagebreak

# Appendix: Key R Code

## R0 Scaling

```r
# SPR with plus group at maxage (MSEtool convention)
l_spr <- c(1, cumprod(exp(-M_all[-length(M_all)])))
l_spr[length(l_spr)] <- l_spr[length(l_spr)] / (1 - exp(-M_all[length(M_all)]))
SPR_0  <- sum(l_spr * W_all * Mat_all)  # tonnes per virgin recruit
R0_mse <- round(B0 / SPR_0)            # R0 consistent with B0_SS3
```

## Perr_y Construction

```r
build_perr_y <- function(sigmaR_i, AC_i, seed_i = 42) {
  # Pre-history: maxage columns
  Perr_pre  <- matrix(exp(rnorm(n_sim * maxage, 0, 0.1)),
                      nrow = n_sim, ncol = maxage)
  # Projection: AR1 log-normal
  Perr_proj <- sim_perr_proj(n_sim, n_proyear, sigmaR_i, AC_i, seed = seed_i)
  # Combine: [nsim, maxage] | [nsim, nyears] | [nsim, proyears]
  cbind(Perr_pre, Perr_hist, Perr_proj)
}
```

## Proportional HCR

```r
HCR_proportional <- function(x, Data, reps = 1, ...) {
  ind   <- Data@Ind[x, ]
  B_rel <- ind[max(which(!is.na(ind)))]  # last non-NA relative index
  B_abs <- B_rel * B0_MSE                # scale to tonnes
  TAC   <- max(B_abs * 0.05, 0)          # 5% of absolute biomass
  Rec   <- new("Rec")
  Rec@TAC <- rep(TAC, reps)
  return(Rec)
}
class(HCR_proportional) <- "MP"
```
